tax: Loess Siltwalker is placed at species rank beneath Siltwalker (Lithic order, Earth Elementals class). Diagnosis rests on a dry upright silt column, a static-bound surface, and abrasion as its primary harm. It is separated from its sibling the Bentonite Claymaw by its dry drifting column and abrasive attack versus the Claymaw's wet swelling engulfment; the two share the genus body-plan but never the wet engulfment. Composition, not colour or menace, fixes the placement: wind-sorted loess and rock-flour held by static earth-charge.
ap: Loess Siltwalker stands 1.6–2 m tall, a slender shifting column. Its silhouette is an upright spindle of silt, broad at the base, that leans as it walks. Three diagnostic surface markers separate it in the field: a faint crackle and the smell of dry stone before contact; a surface that ripples in slow standing waves like a sand-clock mid-pour; and a grey film it sheds onto everything within a pace, the clearest field sign. It moves with a gliding lean-and-recover drift across flat dust, unable to hold form in a strong cross-draught, and shows none; it dulls lamplight by clouding the air around it. Even after dispersal it can be identified from a clean-swept floor flanked by silt drifts and a grey film on nearby surfaces, so a survey team need not see the living body to log the species.
eco: Loess Siltwalker persists in dry dust shafts, wind-cut loess shelves, and abandoned upper galleries where fine sediment lies undisturbed. It does not breed; it forms where a deep dry loess bed takes a static earth-charge and stands up, and recurs whenever the bed re-charges after the dust resettles, raising the column again. It sustains its mass on static earth-charge in dry sediment, drawing in loose dust drawn up to replace what it sheds. Range is bounded by the still-air pocket it stands in: outside that band the body a strong draught disperses it into a settling cloud. Juvenile or nascent forms appear as a dust shaft where the floor self-sweeps and a film appears on the walls, which lets a surveyor predict where an adult will rise.
beh: Behaviour is the behaviour of its medium given intent. Loess Siltwalker is roused by movement that stirs its dust or a light that warms the air and otherwise lies a tall still drift indistinguishable from a dune. It claims a single still-air dust shaft and answers intrusion with advancing into the intruder's breathing-space to abrade and choke. Toward grey-folk it is slow and avoidable if seen; survey crews report that Mist-Touched scouts wear sealed masks in any shaft showing a self-swept floor. It is not cultural and keeps no craft — its 'decisions' are thresholds of pressure, heat, current, or charge, not plans.
traitsText: Stable characters of Loess Siltwalker: a body of wind-sorted loess and rock-flour held by static earth-charge; immune to piercing and slashing, which pass through it; and a defining weakness — water and strong draught, which knock it down at once. Its senses run through the medium itself: air-pressure and warmth sensing through its dust; blind to still cold objects. It tolerates heat and darkness but fails at any wind over a walking pace, and any wetting. Mass varies with available element, yet the proportions, the upright spindle and shed film, stay constant, which is why mass alone never reclassifies the taxon.
abilities: Abilities of Loess Siltwalker are bounded by its anatomy, not borrowed from other elements: it abrades exposed skin and fouls lungs at close range; it clouds the air to dim lights and blind pursuers; it draws up loose dust to repair and grow; and it can collapse and re-rise a short distance to dodge. Each is a direct expression of static-charged dry sediment standing in still air, so it cannot, for instance, cross water, hold form in wind, or strike with mass. The powers scale with body mass and fade as the body disperses.
bg: In the Throat, Loess Siltwalker matters because it makes still dust shafts a slow suffocation hazard, and the self-swept floor it leaves is now a standard mask-up signal. Grey-folk survey practice treats it as an air-quality hazard logged with dry-shaft routes, and its presence reshapes routes through the upper dust shafts, now ventilated or masked before entry. Its grey film, dated by depth, lets surveyors tell how long a shaft has been still.
g: Loess Siltwalker (Pulvis ambulans) is a dry silt elemental that walks as a slow upright column of bound dust and scours skin and lungs. Documented from the dry dust shafts and wind-cut loess shelves of the upper tiers, it expresses a single inner element as its whole anatomy — a head-high column of fine bound silt held upright by static earth-charge — rather than as flesh laid over an animal skeleton. It is the dry-country sibling of the Claymaw, hunting by abrasion and inhalation rather than engulfment. Field teams record it as a true elemental, not a beast wearing mineral armour: when it disperses it leaves a sudden settling of fine silt that coats every surface in a fingerprint-grey film and no carcass.
